assertionResults.title: Primary navigation exposes required graph destinations and focus states
assertionResults.reason: Desktop and mobile navigation both expose the required BETTER destinations, and the tested actions open graph focus-state hashes rather than leaving users on disconnected document anchors or in a broken mobile overlay.

observations: Desktop navigation exposed What is BETTER, Live Now, Roadmap, Tokenomics, Architecture, Evidence & Sources, and Risks & Caveats, and tested clicks drove the shell into graph hashes such as #graph-roadmap and #graph-tokenomics.

On iPhone 14 emulation, opening the navigation menu showed the same destination set; selecting destinations dismissed the overlay cleanly and landed on the intended graph-focused state instead of trapping the user in the menu.
evidence.consoleErrors: none (console was cleared and checked during the run; no significant runtime errors were observed)

assertionResults.title: The shell immediately reads as a graph-first explorable mindmap
assertionResults.reason: The first meaningful roadmap exploration view visibly presents an interactive graph shell with multiple node buttons, an active focal state, and explicit overview/orientation controls, so it reads as an explorable atlas rather than a plain linear document.

observations: The graph overview shows eight explorable BETTER nodes in one shell: What is BETTER, Proof & Trust, Live Now, Roadmap, Tokenomics, Architecture, Evidence & Sources, and Risks & Caveats.
The focused roadmap state is visually obvious through the active node treatment plus visible orientation affordances such as the overview control and breadcrumb/wayfinding labels.

assertionResults.title: Graph wayfinding and orientation recovery stay available
assertionResults.reason: Traversal between roadmap-adjacent graph surfaces preserved visible orientation cues and recovery controls, allowing the user to move across focused states and return to overview without losing context.

observations: The shell kept visible wayfinding cues during traversal, including the graph breadcrumb plus explicit recovery controls labeled Return to overview / Back to overview.
Recorded traversal from Roadmap to Tokenomics to Architecture and back to overview completed without stranding the user in a lost state or removing the surrounding graph context.

assertionResults.title: Supporting scroll/story state stays synchronized without becoming the primary navigation model
assertionResults.reason: Scrolling through surrounding narrative content did not hijack or desynchronize the graph shell's active focus, while explicit graph/navigation actions remained the mechanism for changing major exploration states.

observations: During the recorded forward/back scroll check, moving through the hero and surrounding story sections did not silently flip the graph to unrelated roadmap states or force progression through the roadmap via scrolling.
Major transitions still happened through deliberate top-nav or node/focus actions, demonstrating that scrolling supports the experience without replacing node-first graph navigation.

assertionResults.title: Node-first exploration is the primary interaction model
assertionResults.reason: Across desktop and mobile, the shell foregrounds graph node selection and focused detail-surface exploration, so interaction reads as atlas traversal rather than section-by-section anchor browsing.

observations: At rest, the graph overview presents the node map itself as the main exploration affordance before any linear page reading is required.

Selecting a node opens an in-shell focused surface with roadmap detail while preserving graph context, and the mobile run likewise reaches roadmap focus through the graph shell rather than a simple document-anchor jump.

assertionResults.title: Major exploration transitions do not require linear page scrolling
assertionResults.reason: Users can jump directly between major roadmap-adjacent states through the graph shell and top navigation controls, without having to linearly scroll through the page to advance the experience.

observations: The recorded transition flow moved directly between major states such as Roadmap, Tokenomics, and Architecture via explicit controls instead of relying on incremental document scrolling.
The major-state controls remain visible while focused surfaces are open, keeping non-linear movement available throughout the exploration flow.

frictions.description: Automation encountered duplicate accessible button names such as 'Tokenomics' because both overview nodes and focused-surface related links were simultaneously visible.
frictions.resolution: Re-ran the affected recordings using stable element refs from fresh snapshots instead of ambiguous semantic locators.

frictions.description: The first mobile navigation recording timed out before the 'Open navigation menu' interaction completed.
frictions.resolution: Discarded the partial take and captured a complete replacement recording as `VAL-NARR-004-mobile-nav-destination-flow-v2.webm`.

summary.note: All 6 assigned roadmap-core assertions passed in the browser run. Desktop and mobile navigation opened graph focus states cleanly, the shell read as a graph-first atlas with durable wayfinding, node-first exploration remained primary, scrolling did not become the navigation model, and major roadmap transitions were reachable without linear page scrolling. No significant browser runtime errors were observed.
